nodes.label: Information Security Management
footnotes: GRC Core
Objective: protect information assets

nodes.label: Standards
footnotes: Applicable and certifiable norms and requirements

nodes.label: Cyber Incident Management
footnotes: Cyber incident: event or set of events affecting the CIA of a system's information

nodes.label: Continuity Plans
footnotes:
Objective:
- Provide fast and effective response to incidents and recover normal operations
- Reduce decision-making

Helps to:
- Maintain service level
- Define recovery period
- Analysis and improvement

nodes.label:
Applicable
Legislation
footnotes: Mandatory laws and regulations

nodes.label: HLS (High Level Structure)
footnotes:
HLS: The standard follows a High Level Structure
This allows integration with other ISO standards and content reuse

This is the case for ISO 9001 (Quality) and ISO 27001 (Information Security)
They can be integrated into a single management system

nodes.label: Audits
footnotes:
Internal
External (certification) - 3 years
Follow-up - 1/2 years

nodes.label: PDCA Cycle
footnotes:
PDCA Loop / Deming Cycle
- Plan: sections 4-7; scope, roles, risks
- Do: section 8; Deploy, Implement
- Check: section 9; measure effectiveness, internal audit
- Act: section 10; improvements, corrections

nodes.label:
ISO 27001
(certifiable)
footnotes:
Versions (1-2-3):
- Reduction of controls
- Inclusion of HLS
- Flexibility
- Risk-based approach
- Introduction of the Deming cycle

Project start:
- Management commitment
- Timeline: 1/2 to 2 years (depends on size)
- Team:
  - Manager
  - Sponsor - Supporting executive
  - Systems manager

Sections:
- Introductory:
  - 0. Introduction
  - 1. Scope
  - 2. Normative references
  - 3. Terms and definitions
- Mandatory:
  - 4. Organizational context
  - 5. Leadership
  - 6. Planning (Information security risk assessment)
  - 7. Support (including Resources)
  - 8. Operation (Information security risk treatment)
  - 9. Performance evaluation
  - 10. Improvement

nodes.label:
ISO 27002
(best practices)
footnotes: 93 controls

Control groups:
- Organizational
- People
- Physical (tangible assets)
- Technological
Extra: Annexes - to adapt from version to version

Control attributes:
- Type: preventive (before), detective (during), corrective (after)
- Dimensions: CIA
- Cybersecurity concept: mappable to NIST
- Operational capabilities
- Security domain

nodes.label: ISO 27000
footnotes:
27001 (Certifiable standard)
27002 (controls and best practices)
27003 (ISMS implementation)
27004 (security metrics)
27005 (risk management)

nodes.label:
ISO 9001
(Quality)
footnotes: Quality standard
HLS: High Level Structure

nodes.label: Information Security
footnotes:
Threats:
- attack
- environmental
- error
- system failure
Increasingly difficult to manage

nodes.label: ISMS
footnotes:
Definition:
Set of policies and procedures for resources and activities.

Mandatory artifact for ISO 27000:
ISO defines it as part of the overall management system

Objective:
- Protect assets
- Risk-based approach

Difference from Framework (NIST):
- Auditable
- Certifiable
- Formal management rather than best practices
- Defines how to do things, not just what to do

Includes...
- Strategies: Where to start and how to execute a project
- People and their training
- Processes
- Technology

nodes.label: Governance, Risk, Compliance
footnotes: Idea: align business, security and regulations

G: align security with business objectives
R: identify, analyze risks on assets and take measures
C: compliance with internal policies and external laws

nodes.label: ISO 17799
footnotes: This ISO gave rise to ISO 27002

nodes.label:
British Standard
7799
footnotes: Based on security best practices and ISMS implementation

nodes.label: Risk Assessment
footnotes:
Risk/Benefit
Risk management = Analysis + Evaluation + Treatment
Requirements impose Controls protect against Threat exploits Vulnerability affects Asset causes Impact

Define Scope
Identify Assets
Identify Threats
Identify Vulnerabilities - Safeguards
Evaluate Risk
Treat Risk

What needs to be protected?
Confidentiality
Integrity
Availability

nodes.label: MAGERIT (3 books)
footnotes: Considers dependencies between assets

Expands asset dimensions:
- CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)
- Authenticity (is who they claim to be)
- Traceability (who did what)

Risk types:
- Intrinsic to the asset - without security measures
- Frequency (probability) and degradation (impact) ← generated by threat
- Residual: after security measures
- Potential: if adequate measures are not taken
- Accumulated: company total. Sum of asset risks
- Compared against the defined threshold

Emphasis on safeguards:
- Security measure
- Can take different forms
- Incorporated into the asset

nodes.label: AEPD
footnotes: Spanish Data Protection Agency
Responsible for compliance with LOPDGDD and GDPR in Spain

nodes.label: INCIBE
footnotes:
Define acceptable risk level (1-9)
Define:
- Critical assets
- Threats
- Asset-Threat crossings
  - Probability
  - Impact
  - Risk = Impact x Probability

nodes.label:
Asset
name, desc, owner, type, location, critical

Threats
type:
natural origin,
system failure,
human error
attack

Evaluate risk
impact x probability
Scale from 1 to 5

Treat risk
avoid
reduce
transfer - let a third party manage it
accept
SOA
footnotes: Statement of Applicability

Security control selection
Which controls will be applied and which won't
Whether they are already applied

nodes.label: Book 1: method
footnotes:
MAR 1: Asset characterization. Asset value is defined:
- Own/Accumulated (other assets depend on it)
- Quantitative/Qualitative
- A number for C I A Authentication
MAR 2: Threat characterization
MAR 3: Safeguard characterization
MAR 4: Risk estimation

nodes.label: Policy: new concept
footnotes:
Security policy: high level
Topic-based policies: detailed
Rules: instructions

nodes.label: ENX: Supply chain information exchange platform
footnotes:
Evaluates and guarantees information security in the German automotive sector. Evaluates the ISMS.
TISAX is an assessment, not an audit — it gives ratings.

nodes.label: TISAX
footnotes: Assessment and certification standard to demonstrate compliance with information security requirements in the automotive industry

Need for a specific approach
Lack of trust in third-party audits (ISO27001)
Usually required by German automotive companies from their suppliers

nodes.label: TISAX Assessments
footnotes:
Assessment levels:
- 1: self-assessment
- 2: remote assessment
- 3: on-site assessment (3rd party comes to business)

The assessment establishes maturity levels for the assessed entity
Defines must / should on evaluated aspects

nodes.label: IEC 62443
footnotes: IACS: Industrial Automation and Control Systems
Focuses on production lines, networks and production and control components

Industrial security lifecycle phases:
- Assessment: analysis
- Implementation: apply measures
- Maintenance: monitoring and updating

nodes.label: Zones and conduits
footnotes:
Key concepts in industrial cybersecurity:
- Zones: Network separation into zones with the same security level
- Conduits: connections between zones

Security Levels (SL) (1-4): How resistant to attacks
SLT (target): How far we are from the security level per zone.
	SLT = Unmitigated risk / tolerable risk

nodes.label:
ENS
(law/certification)
footnotes: Definition: Establishes security principles, requirements and measures

Objectives:
- Create security conditions
- Promote continuous security management
- Better resilience

Defined security dimensions:
- Confidentiality
- Integrity
- Availability
- Authenticity
- Traceability
- Conservation

nodes.label: Security Principles and Measures
footnotes:
Security must be present at all times and in all areas
Risk-based management
Prevention, detection, response and conservation
Defense in depth
Continuous vigilance
Differentiation of responsibility

75 measures:
- Organizational framework: Global organization (4)
- Operational framework: System operation (31)
- Protection measures: Specific assets (40)

nodes.label: Defined Roles
footnotes:
Information Owner: Establishes information security requirements
Service Owner: Establishes security requirements for services
Security Manager: Maintain security
System Manager: Verifies information system

Defines a Security Committee
- Coordinates security at company level
- Made up of: Security Manager and area representatives

nodes.label: Business Continuity Plan (BCP)
footnotes:
Priorities:
- trust
- people integrity
- client/supplier relationships
- not stopping production
- environment

nodes.label: IT Continuity Plan
footnotes:
Phases:
- 0. Scope
- 1. Organization analysis:
  - Critical services and their assets
  - Risks
  - Impact (BIA, RPO, RTO)
- 2. Strategies: define needs, responses and strategies
- 3. Contingency response (adverse situation):
  - Implement the strategy
  - Measures depending on service criticality
  - Contains:
    - Crisis plan
    - Maintenance plan
    - Test plan
- 4. Test and review plan

nodes.label: Key Concepts
footnotes:
Critical service: If it fails, production stops
- Related assets: DB, server, internet, firewall...

RPO: Data lost
RTO: Time lost

nodes.label: Possible Tools
footnotes:
backup
hardware: onsite, offsite
personnel: avoid single point of failure, remote access
backup centers:
- cold, warm, hot: external infrastructure ready to replace services. The hotter, the faster the deployment
vendors with short-term agreements

nodes.label: IRP: Cyber Incident Response Plan
footnotes:
Objectives:
- Replicability
- Speed:
  - Detection
  - Response
- Compliance
Required by ISO 27002

nodes.label: Playbook
footnotes:
Document defining what to do in each IRP phase
A playbook can be created per threat

nodes.label: Ransomware
footnotes:
Concept:
- Encrypt files
- Move through the network and encrypt other devices
- Demand ransom

Extra type: RansomwareaaS

nodes.label: Constitution 1978 (Article 18)
footnotes:
right to privacy
privacy of communications

nodes.label: Corporate Email
footnotes:
Evidence nullity due to lack of prior notice
Employees must be informed of workplace monitoring

nodes.label: Communication Privacy
footnotes: Judicial authorization required to violate the right

nodes.label: Right to Privacy
footnotes: Legitimate reason required to violate the right

nodes.label:
Police Criminal Procedure Act
The police trojan
footnotes: A judge may introduce malware into an accused's devices to gather evidence (serious crimes)

nodes.label: General Telecommunications Act
footnotes: Legal interception of communications in extreme cases: lives at risk
Greater rights for users

nodes.label: Information Society Services Act
footnotes:
Applies to internet services:
- ecommerce, web
- spam consent
- cookie usage
Example: blocking of La Liga IP addresses. Requires judicial authorization

nodes.label:
NIS 2 (Network & Information Security)
EU Directive
footnotes:
Impacted companies:
- essential services
- service providers
Law per country

Improvements:
- Broader scope: inclusion of sectors and new technologies (AI)
- Essential entities (already existed)
- Important entities (newly added)
- Supply chain consideration
- Incident notification
- Supervision and sanctions
- Management board responsibility
- Training
- Personal sanctions / Temporary disqualification

nodes.label: PCI-DSS EU Directive
footnotes:
Impacts:
- financial entities
- companies storing card data

Main concepts:
- secure network
- protect data
- vulnerability management
- access control measures

nodes.label: PSD2
footnotes:
Objectives:
- protect data
- customer security
- unify providers
- include fintechs

Measures:
- SCA (Strong Customer Authentication)
- MFA
- verifications

nodes.label: DORA
footnotes: Security and resilience of financial services

nodes.label: elDAS
footnotes: Unified and secure digital ID in the EU

nodes.label: CRA (Cyber Resilience Act)
footnotes:
Objectives:
- Better market security
- CE marking
- Vulnerability management
- Transparency with customers and suppliers
- Reporting
Mandatory from 11 December 2027

nodes.label: Artifacts
footnotes: Risk analysis
SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) and VEX (Vulnerability Exploitability eXchange)
Vulnerability management plan
Technical conformity documentation
Security update policy

nodes.label:
GDPR
(EU regulation/law)
footnotes: Applies to manual and automated data processing; each processing activity has its own controller
Consent must be given through a clear positive action (not the absence of a negative). Example: an unchecked checkbox

Does not apply to deceased persons
Does not apply to personal or domestic documents (e.g. personal agenda)
Key role: DPO - Data Protection Officer
- Legal knowledge
- No conflict of interests
- Responsible for data protection compliance

nodes.label: DPIA
footnotes: Main GDPR artifact: Data Protection Impact Assessment

Must include:
- Description of the processing
- Assessment of necessity and proportionality
- Assessment of risks to individuals' rights and freedoms
- Measures to mitigate risks

nodes.label: Personal data
footnotes:
Personal data categories:
- personal
- biometric
- genetic
- health-related

nodes.label: Anonymization and pseudonymization
footnotes:
Pseudonymization: data not attributable to a person without additional information.
Ex: two tables: id, age; id, name. Only the first table is pseudonymized.
Anonymization: one table: age, job. Irreversible, cannot be attributed to a person

nodes.label: Basque and Catalan
footnotes:
Data protection laws of each autonomous community
Apply to companies operating in that community

edges.label:
security in services
sold in the market
